result:
# Notes for the Quiet

I woke this morning to a quiet that felt like snow. It wasn’t cold or hushed; it was generous. Birdsong threaded itself between the hum of the refrigerator and the soft click of the radiator. I poured water into a chipped mug and watched steam curl like a question mark.

There is a particular kind of morning that asks nothing and offers everything. No inbox demands, no calendar alarm, just the day’s edges waiting for you to draw them. I carried my coffee to the window and watched the neighbor’s dog trot past, nose to the grass, committing tiny discoveries to memory. It made me think of how we forget to smell things.

When was the last time you noticed bread cooling, or the way light lands on a bookshelf differently every hour? I used to chase big feelings—mountains, thunderstorms, first kisses. I still love them. But today the quiet made a small altar of ordinary time. I read a sentence, then read it again, not for meaning but for music.

Afternoon came with the smell of wet pavement, and I took a slow walk, picking up the stray thoughts I usually ignore. I found a penny, a receipt for milk, a leaf glued to the curb with stubborn dew. I pocketed none of it. The quiet had done its work: a little wider space, a steadier breath, the sense that I could be generous with patience, like the morning had been with me.

I wrote the day’s first sentence in my head, then let it go. The real story was living without punctuation.

I woke this morning to a quiet that felt like snow. It wasn’t cold or hushed; it was generous. Birdsong threaded itself between the hum of the refrigerator and the soft click of the radiator. I poured water into a chipped mug and watched steam curl like a question mark.

I carried my coffee to the window and watched the neighbor’s dog trot past, nose to the grass, committing tiny discoveries to memory. It made me think of how we forget to smell things.

When was the last time you noticed bread cooling, or the way light lands on a bookshelf differently every hour? I used to chase big feelings—mountains, thunderstorms, first kisses. I still love them. But today the quiet made a small altar of ordinary time. I read a sentence, then read it again, not for meaning but for music.

Afternoon came with the smell of wet pavement, and I took a slow walk, picking up the stray thoughts I usually ignore. I found a penny, a receipt for milk, a leaf glued to the curb with stubborn dew. I pocketed none of it. The quiet had done its work: a little wider space, a steadier breath, the sense that I could be generous with patience, like the morning had been with me.

I wrote the day’s first sentence in my head, then let it go. The real story was living without punctuation.
